title: भगवद्गीता अध्याय ४ - ज्ञानकर्मसन्न्यासयोग
titleEn: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 4 - Jnana Karma Sannyasa Yoga
devanagariText:
॥ अथ चतुर्थोऽध्यायः - ज्ञानकर्मसन्न्यासयोगः ॥

श्रीभगवानुवाच —
इमं विवस्वते योगं प्रोक्तवानहमव्ययम्।
विवस्वान्मनवे प्राह मनुरिक्ष्वाकवेऽब्रवीत्॥१॥

एवं परम्पराप्राप्तमिमं राजर्षयो विदुः।
स कालेनेह महता योगो नष्टः परन्तप॥२॥

स एवायं मया तेऽद्य योगः प्रोक्तः पुरातनः।
भक्तोऽसि मे सखा चेति रहस्यं ह्येतदुत्तमम्॥३॥

अर्जुन उवाच —
अपरं भवतो जन्म परं जन्म विवस्वतः।
कथमेतद्विजानीयां त्वमादौ प्रोक्तवानिति॥४॥

श्रीभगवानुवाच —
बहूनि मे व्यतीतानि जन्मानि तव चार्जुन।
तान्यहं वेद सर्वाणि न त्वं वेत्थ परन्तप॥५॥

अजोऽपि सन्नव्ययात्मा भूतानामीश्वरोऽपि सन्।
प्रकृतिं स्वामधिष्ठाय सम्भवाम्यात्ममायया॥६॥

यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत।
अभ्युत्थानमधर्मस्य तदात्मानं सृजाम्यहम्॥७॥

परित्राणाय साधूनां विनाशाय च दुष्कृताम्।
धर्मसंस्थापनार्थाय सम्भवामि युगे युगे॥८॥

जन्म कर्म च मे दिव्यमेवं यो वेत्ति तत्त्वतः।
त्यक्त्वा देहं पुनर्जन्म नैति मामेति सोऽर्जुन॥९॥

वीतरागभयक्रोधा मन्मया मामुपाश्रिताः।
बहवो ज्ञानतपसा पूता मद्भावमागताः॥१०॥

ये यथा मां प्रपद्यन्ते तांस्तथैव भजाम्यहम्।
मम वर्त्मानुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः पार्थ सर्वशः॥११॥

काङ्क्षन्तः कर्मणां सिद्धिं यजन्त इह देवताः।
क्षिप्रं हि मानुषे लोके सिद्धिर्भवति कर्मजा॥१२॥

चातुर्वर्ण्यं मया सृष्टं गुणकर्मविभागशः।
तस्य कर्तारमपि मां विद्ध्यकर्तारमव्ययम्॥१३॥

न मां कर्माणि लिम्पन्ति न मे कर्मफले स्पृहा।
इति मां योऽभिजानाति कर्मभिर्न स बध्यते॥१४॥

एवं ज्ञात्वा कृतं कर्म पूर्वैरपि मुमुक्षुभिः।
कुरु कर्मैव तस्मात्त्वं पूर्वैः पूर्वतरं कृतम्॥१५॥

किं कर्म किमकर्मेति कवयोऽप्यत्र मोहिताः।
तत्ते कर्म प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वा मोक्ष्यसेऽशुभात्॥१६॥

कर्मणो ह्यपि बोद्धव्यं बोद्धव्यं च विकर्मणः।
अकर्मणश्च बोद्धव्यं गहना कर्मणो गतिः॥१७॥

कर्मण्यकर्म यः पश्येदकर्मणि च कर्म यः।
स बुद्धिमान्मनुष्येषु स युक्तः कृत्स्नकर्मकृत्॥१८॥

यस्य सर्वे समारम्भाः कामसङ्कल्पवर्जिताः।
ज्ञानाग्निदग्धकर्माणं तमाहुः पण्डितं बुधाः॥१९॥

त्यक्त्वा कर्मफलासङ्गं नित्यतृप्तो निराश्रयः।
कर्मण्यभिप्रवृत्तोऽपि नैव किञ्चित्करोति सः॥२०॥

श्रद्धावाँल्लभते ज्ञानं तत्परः संयतेन्द्रियः।
ज्ञानं लब्ध्वा परां शान्तिमचिरेणाधिगच्छति॥३९॥

अज्ञश्चाश्रद्दधानश्च संशयात्मा विनश्यति।
नायं लोकोऽस्ति न परो न सुखं संशयात्मनः॥४०॥

योगसन्न्यस्तकर्माणं ज्ञानसञ्छिन्नसंशयम्।
आत्मवन्तं न कर्माणि निबध्नन्ति धनञ्जय॥४१॥

तस्मादज्ञानसम्भूतं हृत्स्थं ज्ञानासिनात्मनः।
छित्त्वैनं संशयं योगमातिष्ठोत्तिष्ठ भारत॥४२॥

॥ इति श्रीमद्भगवद्गीतासूपनिषत्सु ब्रह्मविद्यायां योगशास्त्रे
श्रीकृष्णार्जुनसंवादे ज्ञानकर्मसन्न्यासयोगो नाम चतुर्थोऽध्यायः ॥
transliteration:
|| Atha Chaturtho'dhyayah - Jnana Karma Sannyasa Yogah ||

Shri Bhagavan Uvacha —
Imam Vivasvate Yogam Proktavan-Aham-Avyayam.
Vivasvan Manave Praha Manur-Ikshvakave'bravit.||1||

Evam Parampara-Praptam-Imam Rajarshayo Viduh.
Sa Kalene-Ha Mahata Yogo Nashtah Parantapa.||2||

Sa Eva-Ayam Maya Te'dya Yogah Proktah Puratanah.
Bhakto'si Me Sakha Che-Ti Rahasyam Hy-Etad-Uttamam.||3||

Arjuna Uvacha —
Aparam Bhavato Janma Param Janma Vivasvatah.
Katham-Etad Vijaniyam Tvam-Adau Proktavan-Iti.||4||

Shri Bhagavan Uvacha —
Bahuni Me Vyatitani Janmani Tava Cha-Arjuna.
Tany-Aham Veda Sarvani Na Tvam Vettha Parantapa.||5||

Ajo'pi Sann-Avyaya-Atma Bhutanam-Ishvaro'pi San.
Prakritim Svam-Adhishthaya Sambhavamy-Atma-Mayaya.||6||

Yada Yada Hi Dharmasya Glanir-Bhavati Bharata.
Abhyutthanam-Adharmasya Tada-Atmanam Srijamy-Aham.||7||

Paritranaya Sadhunam Vinashaya Cha Dushkritam.
Dharma-Samsthapana-Arthaya Sambhavami Yuge Yuge.||8||

Janma Karma Cha Me Divyam-Evam Yo Vetti Tattvatah.
Tyaktva Deham Punar-Janma Naiti Mam-Eti So'rjuna.||9||

Vita-Raga-Bhaya-Krodha Man-Maya Mam-Upashritah.
Bahavo Jnana-Tapasa Puta Mad-Bhavam-Agatah.||10||

Ye Yatha Mam Prapadyante Tams-Tathaiva Bhajamy-Aham.
Mama Vartma-Anuvartante Manushyah Partha Sarvashah.||11||

Kankshantah Karmanam Siddhim Yajanta Iha Devatah.
Kshipram Hi Manushe Loke Siddhir-Bhavati Karma-Ja.||12||

Chatur-Varnyam Maya Srishtam Guna-Karma-Vibhagashah.
Tasya Kartaram-Api Mam Viddhy-Akartaram-Avyayam.||13||

Na Mam Karmani Limpanti Na Me Karma-Phale Spriha.
Iti Mam Yo'bhijanati Karmabhir-Na Sa Badhyate.||14||

Evam Jnatva Kritam Karma Purvair-Api Mumukshubhih.
Kuru Karma-Iva Tasmat-Tvam Purvaih Purvataram Kritam.||15||

Kim Karma Kim-Akarme-Ti Kavayo'py-Atra Mohitah.
Tat-Te Karma Pravakshyami Yaj-Jnatva Mokshyase'shubhat.||16||

Karmano Hy-Api Boddhavyam Boddhavyam Cha Vikarmanah.
Akarmanash-Cha Boddhavyam Gahana Karmano Gatih.||17||

Karmany-Akarma Yah Pashyed-Akarmani Cha Karma Yah.
Sa Buddhiman-Manushyeshu Sa Yuktah Kritsna-Karma-Krit.||18||

Yasya Sarve Samarambhah Kama-Sankalpa-Varjitah.
Jnana-Agni-Dagdha-Karmanam Tam-Ahuh Panditam Budhah.||19||

Shraddha-Van Labhate Jnanam Tat-Parah Samyate-Indriyah.
Jnanam Labdhva Param Shantim-Achirena-Adhigachchhati.||39||

Tasmad-Ajnana-Sambhutam Hrit-Stham Jnana-Asina-Atmanah.
Chhittva-Inam Samshayam Yogam-Atishtha-Uttishtha Bharata.||42||

|| Iti Shrimad-Bhagavad-Gitasu-Upanishatsu Brahma-Vidyayam Yoga-Shastre
Shri-Krishna-Arjuna-Samvade Jnana-Karma-Sannyasa-Yogo Nama Chaturtho'dhyayah ||
hindiMeaning: चतुर्थ अध्याय 'ज्ञानकर्मसन्न्यासयोग' में श्रीकृष्ण बताते हैं कि यह योग उन्होंने सबसे पहले सूर्य (विवस्वान) को बताया था, फिर परंपरा से राजर्षियों तक पहुँचा। अर्जुन के पूछने पर कृष्ण अवतार का रहस्य बताते हैं — 'यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति' — जब-जब धर्म की हानि होती है, तब-तब मैं प्रकट होता हूँ। साधुओं के रक्षण, दुष्टों के विनाश और धर्म की स्थापना के लिए मैं युग-युग में जन्म लेता हूँ। फिर कर्म, अकर्म और विकर्म का सूक्ष्म विवेचन किया गया है। विभिन्न प्रकार के यज्ञों का वर्णन है। अंत में कहते हैं कि ज्ञान की तलवार से संशय को काटकर योग में स्थित होकर उठो।
benefits: Reveals the secret of divine incarnation (Avatar Rahasya)
Contains the most famous verse: Yada Yada Hi Dharmasya
Teaches that knowledge burns all karmic bondage like fire
Explains the relationship between action, inaction, and forbidden action
Invokes faith and devotion through understanding god's divine play
Traditionally recited for removal of doubts through the sword of knowledge
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Krishna, Bhagavad Gita Chapter 4 - Jnana Karma Sannyasa Yoga (भगवद्गीता अध्याय ४ - ज्ञानकर्मसन्न्यासयोग) stands as a sacred prayer. Attributed to Bhagavad Gita - Mahabharata, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to explain the relationship between action, inaction, and forbidden action as well as destroy doubts through the sword of knowledge. Additionally, it is known to grant faith and devotion through understanding God's divine play. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Wednesday (Budhvar) and during Janmashtami. Comprising 42 verses, it can be completed in approximately 18 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Bhagavad Gita Chapter 4 - Jnana Karma Sannyasa Yoga in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is Bhagavad Gita Chapter 4 — Jnana Karma Sannyasa Yoga?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 4 (भगवद्गीता अध्याय ४), titled Jnana Karma Sannyasa Yoga (the Yoga of Knowledge and the Renunciation of Action), is the fourth chapter of the Srimad Bhagavad Gita and contains 42 verses. It opens with Krishna revealing the ancient guru-parampara (lineage) through which the Yoga he is now teaching was transmitted from Surya (Vivasvan) to Manu to Ikshvaku and the rajarshis (4.1-3), then makes the great avatara declaration (4.5-8) — that he himself is the eternal Lord who incarnates from age to age to protect dharma. The chapter teaches the relationship between action, inaction, and forbidden action (4.16-18); lists twelve forms of yajna (4.25-30); exalts jnana-yajna as the supreme; teaches the approach to a guru by prostration, questioning and service (4.34); and concludes that the sword of jnana (jnana-asina) destroys all doubts and karma (4.42). (Source: Srimad Bhagavad Gita Chapter 4; Mahabharata Bhishma Parva.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous Yada yada hi dharmasya verse in Chapter 4?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 4.7-4.8 contain the most quoted avatara-declaration in all Hindu scripture: Yada yada hi dharmasya glanir-bhavati Bharata, abhyutthanam-adharmasya tadatmanam srijamy-aham — Whenever there is decline of dharma, O Bharata, and rise of adharma, then I send forth Myself. Verse 4.8: Paritranaya sadhunam vinashaya cha dushkritam, dharma-samsthapanarthaya sambhavami yuge yuge — For the protection of the good, for the destruction of evil-doers, for the establishment of dharma, I come into being age after age. These two verses are the foundational avatara-shastra of Hindu theology — the doctrine that the Supreme Lord descends in many ages and many forms (the Dashavatara of Vishnu — Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha and the future Kalki) for the protection of dharma. The verses are recited at every Vaishnava utsava and quoted in nearly every Hindu discourse. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 4.7-4.8.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the doctrine of divya-janma-karma (divine birth and action) of Krishna in Chapter 4?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 4.5-4.6 give the divya-janma-karma teaching: Bahuni me vyatitani janmani tava cha-Arjuna, tany-aham veda sarvani na tvam vettha Parantapa — Many births of Mine have passed, and yours also, O Arjuna; I know them all, but you do not, O scorcher of foes. Verse 4.6: Ajo'pi sann-avyayatma bhutanam-ishvaro'pi san, prakritim svam-adhishthaya sambhavamy-atmamayaya — Though I am unborn and of imperishable nature, though I am the Lord of all beings, by adhishthana of my own prakriti I take birth through my own atma-maya (divine yogic power). Verse 4.9: Janma karma cha me divyam evam yo vetti tattvatah, tyaktva deham punar-janma naiti mam-eti so'rjuna — One who knows in this manner the divine birth and divine work of mine attains me on leaving this body and is not born again. This is one of the principal moksha-mantras of the Gita and the foundational verse for the Vaishnava doctrine of Bhagavan-tattva (the nature of the personal Lord). (Source: Bhagavad Gita 4.5-4.9.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Brahmarpanam Brahma Havih bhojan-mantra from Chapter 4?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 4.24 is the supreme bhojan-mantra (food-offering mantra) chanted by Hindus before every meal: Brahmarpanam Brahma havir Brahmagnau Brahmana hutam, Brahmaiva tena gantavyam Brahma-karma-samadhina — The act of offering is Brahman, the oblation is Brahman, offered by Brahman in the fire of Brahman; Brahman alone is to be reached by one absorbed in Brahman as action. The verse teaches that for the karma-yogi who has realised that all is Brahman, every act of eating becomes a sacred yajna in which the sacrificer, the offered food, the digestive fire, the offering itself, and the goal — all are Brahman. The verse is recited universally before meals across India in Smarta, Vaishnava, Madhva, Pushtimarga and Lingayat traditions, often combined with Aham vaishvanaro bhutva (Bhagavad Gita 15.14). It transforms the daily act of eating into a Brahma-yajna and is one of the most popular verses memorised by Hindu children. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 4.24; classical Hindu bhojan-vidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the twelve types of yajna listed in Chapter 4?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 4.25-4.30 systematically list twelve forms of yajna (sacrifice/spiritual offering): (1) Daiva-yajna — offerings to the gods. (2) Brahma-yajna — offering of the Atman in the fire of Brahman by the contemplative. (3) Indriya-yajna — offering of the senses in the fire of self-restraint. (4) Vishaya-yajna — offering of sense-objects in the fire of the senses. (5) Atma-samyama-yoga — offering all the functions of the senses and prana in the fire of self-control kindled by knowledge. (6) Dravya-yajna — offering of material wealth (charity). (7) Tapo-yajna — sacrifice of austerity. (8) Yoga-yajna — sacrifice of yogic discipline. (9) Svadhyaya-yajna — sacrifice of Vedic study. (10) Jnana-yajna — sacrifice of knowledge. (11) Pranayama-yajna — sacrifice of prana into apana and apana into prana, of inhalation and exhalation, regulating the flow of vital airs. (12) Ahara-niyama (food-restraint as yajna) — the fasting yogi who offers prana into prana. Krishna concludes (4.33): Shreyan dravya-mayad-yajnaj jnana-yajnah Parantapa — The yajna of knowledge is superior to the yajna of material wealth. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 4.25-4.33.)

seoFaqs.question: What does Chapter 4 teach about approaching a guru?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 4.34 gives the foundational Hindu guru-shastra: Tad-viddhi pranipatena pariprashnena sevaya, upadekshyanti te jnanam jnaninas-tattva-darshinah — Know that (knowledge) by prostration (pranipata), by questioning (pariprashna), and by service (seva); the wise who have realised the Truth will instruct you in knowledge. The three-step approach (1) pranipata — humble prostration before the guru, (2) pariprashna — sincere questioning with the spirit of genuine inquiry, and (3) seva — selfless service of the guru — is the universal Hindu prescription for receiving authentic spiritual knowledge. Verse 4.35 promises: Knowing this, you shall not again be subject to delusion; you shall see all beings without exception in your Self and then in Me. Verse 4.36 declares that even if one is the worst sinner of all sinners, one will cross over all evil with the boat of jnana. The chapter closes (4.42) with the celebrated injunction: Tasmad-ajnana-sambhutam hrit-stham jnana-asina-atmanah, chittva-enam samshayam yogam-atishtho-ttishtha Bharata — Therefore, with the sword of knowledge cut asunder this doubt of the heart born of ignorance; resort to yoga; arise, O Bharata. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 4.34-4.42.)

seoFaqs.question: When is Bhagavad Gita Chapter 4 traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Chapter 4 is recited on Day 4 of an 18-day Gita Parayana cycle. It is especially recited for understanding the divine avatara doctrine, for guidance in approaching a guru, for the consecration of meals (verse 4.24), and for resolving inner doubts about the path of action and renunciation. Specific occasions include: Wednesday (Budhvar — Krishna's day), Ekadashi, Gita Jayanti (Margashirsha Shukla Ekadashi), Janmashtami (the avatara-declaration verses 4.7-8 are central to this festival), Rama Navami and Krishna Jayanti (when the avatara doctrine is celebrated), Guru Purnima (the chapter's guru-upasana teaching is foundational), and before every meal (Brahmarpanam mantra 4.24). The chapter is also chanted as the supreme jnana-prarthana (prayer for knowledge) — the closing verse that the sword of jnana cuts all doubts is recited as a daily affirmation by jijnasus (seekers). (Sources: Gita Mahatmya; classical Hindu guru-shishya parampara tradition; bhojan-vidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Chapter 4 connect to the verse-by-verse Gita pages on this site?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 4 — Jnana Karma Sannyasa Yoga contains 42 verses, each studied verse-by-verse on this site at /gita/chapter-4/verse-1 through /gita/chapter-4/verse-42. The verse-level pages provide the Devanagari, transliteration, word-by-word meaning, English translation, Hindi anvaya and commentary by classical acharyas (Shankara, Ramanuja, Madhva, Gyaneshwar) for each shloka — particularly important for the avatara declaration (4.5-9), the divine birth-and-work moksha-mantra (4.9), the Brahmarpanam mantra (4.24), the twelve yajnas (4.25-30), the guru-upasana verse (4.34), and the jnana-asina concluding verses (4.36-42). This stotra page presents the entire chapter as a single text for parayana — recommended for those approaching a guru, for celebrants of avatara-festivals, and as a daily karma-jnana integration practice. The chapter forms the bridge between Chapter 3 (pure Karma Yoga) and Chapter 5 (Karma Sannyasa Yoga). (Source: Stotra and Gita parayana traditions.)
